hotLead.name: Hot Lead — Ready to Book
hotLead.subject: Your marble restoration — next step: site assessment in {{city}}
hotLead.body:
Hi {{name}},

Thank you for reaching out to Master Sanctum Restoration. From what you've described — {{stoneType}}, {{primaryIssue}}, and your {{timeline}} — this is exactly the kind of commission we assess and restore under conservation protocol.

What I understood:
• Surface: {{surface}}
• Location: {{location}}
• Main concern: {{primaryIssue}}
• Your goal: {{goal}}

Recommended next step: a brief on-site assessment (typically 30–45 minutes). We document substrate, veining, prior repairs, and exposure before any tool touches stone.

To confirm your visit, I need:
1. Best date/time windows (2–3 options)
2. Property address and access notes
3. Photos of the surface if you have them (wide + close-up of worst areas)

Once confirmed, you'll receive a calendar invite and our intake questionnaire ({{intakeUrl}}). After the visit, you'll get a written scope and estimate — no surprise shortcuts, no haste.

Would {{slot1}} or {{slot2}} work for you?

Protecting What Endures,
{{gatekeeperName}}
Master Sanctum Restoration

coldInquiry.name: Cold Inquiry — Education First
coldInquiry.subject: Re: your stone restoration question — happy to help
coldInquiry.body:
Hi {{name}},

Thanks for contacting Master Sanctum. Even if you're still in the research phase, I'm glad to point you in the right direction.

Quick context: most stone issues (etching, dullness, staining, minor scratches) can often be reversed with proper honing and sealing — but the right protocol depends on the stone type and what's already been done to it. Guessing from photos alone can lead to the wrong approach.

A few questions so I can give you useful guidance:
1. What type of stone is it? (marble, granite, travertine, etc.)
2. Where is it located? (kitchen, bath, floor, exterior)
3. What's bothering you most — appearance, feel, or a specific stain/etch?
4. Approximate size or a photo of the area?

No pressure to book today. If it makes sense after that, we can schedule a no-obligation assessment. If it's a simple maintenance question, I'll tell you honestly.

What would be most helpful — a quick call or email back with those details?

Best,
{{gatekeeperName}}
Master Sanctum Restoration

priceShopper.name: Price Shopper — Set Expectations
priceShopper.subject: Re: ballpark pricing for stone restoration
priceShopper.body:
Hi {{name}},

I appreciate you asking upfront — restoration pricing isn't one-size-fits-all, and I want to give you an honest range instead of a number that misleads you.

What drives cost:
• Stone type and finish level (honed vs. polished)
• Square footage and edge detail
• Severity (light etch vs. deep damage, cracks, lippage)
• Access, occupancy, and seal/cure time
• Whether prior repairs need correction first

Typical ranges (Florida residential, for context only — not a quote):
• Single vanity / small countertop: often $800–$2,500
• Full kitchen counter run: often $2,500–$6,500
• Floor or multi-room architectural work: custom scope

To earn a real number, we start with assessment — documented condition, written scope, then estimate. That protects you from paying for work you don't need, or missing damage that would fail later.

If you're comparing bids, ask each provider:
• Do they assess before quoting?
• Hand-finish or machine-only pass?
• What sealer and maintenance protocol do they document?

If you'd like a firm range for your project, send stone type, approximate size, 2–3 photos, and city — I'll tell you whether we're in range and if a visit makes sense.

Fair enough?

{{gatekeeperName}}
Master Sanctum Restoration

siteVisitConfirmation.name: Site Visit Confirmation
siteVisitConfirmation.subject: Confirmed — Master Sanctum site assessment · {{date}} at {{time}}
siteVisitConfirmation.body:
Hi {{name}},

Your on-site stone assessment is confirmed.

Date: {{date}}
Time: {{time}} {{timezone}}
Duration: ~30–45 minutes
Address: {{address}}
Assessor: {{assessor}}
Gatekeeper contact: {{phone}} · {{email}}

What we'll do on site:
• Document stone type, finish, and condition
• Note etching, staining, cracks, lippage, and prior repairs
• Discuss timeline, access, and realistic restoration outcomes
• Outline next steps for written scope and estimate

Please before we arrive:
• Clear the work area as much as practical
• Have photos of damage handy if you took any earlier
• Note any HOA, building, or pet access requirements

If you need to reschedule: reply or call at least 24 hours ahead.

Attached: Client Intake Questionnaire (if not yet returned) — {{intakeUrl}}

We look forward to meeting you and assessing what the stone remembers.

Protecting What Endures,
{{gatekeeperName}}
Master Sanctum Restoration
Guarded by Marmorax
